summary: Justin Brennan teaches beginners how to start multifamily investing, targeting 10-30 unit boutique properties as first deals. He covers deal sourcing via brokers, underwriting metrics (14-20% IRR, 6-10% CoC, 1.8x equity multiple), and capital raising structures using general/limited partner models. He demonstrates how to raise capital through pitch decks and relationships rather than direct seller sourcing.
takeaways: Target 10-30 unit 'boutique' properties for first deal, not 50+ units—easier to finance, raise capital, and manage as a beginner
Use 'on-market to off-market' method: tour listed properties to build broker relationships and access pre-market deals not on websites
Deal must produce IRR 14-20%+, cash-on-cash return 6-10%+, and equity multiple 1.8x or higher—all three metrics required or don't do the deal
General partner brings 10% of capital but receives 30-40% of deal profits; limited partners bring 90% and receive 70%
Acquisition fees, asset management fees (monthly), and cash distributions provide 5-8 different payment streams throughout deal lifecycle
Close escrow in 60-90 days; have 14-day lag after acceptance plus 3 months to raise capital—plenty of time if deal is bite-sized
Physical out-of-pocket can be minimal ($18K example on $1.5M deal) via acquisition fees and refinancing; leverage OPM to scale capital efficiency

skill_candidates.change: Systematically screen deals against three mandatory return metrics before pursuing
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin explicitly teaches that deals must hit IRR 14-20%+, CoC 6-10%+, and 1.8x equity multiple—'if it does not produce the information to the right you don't do the deal'

skill_candidates.change: Build broker relationships by touring on-market properties in target submarket to access pre-market deal flow
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin walks through calling broker Kyle on a listed 12-unit deal to set up tours, establishing rapport to receive pre-market listings within 3 weeks of visiting the market

skill_candidates.change: Pitch deals as investor opportunities (not money-asking) with templated pitch decks covering minimum investment, returns, timeline, and investor distributions
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin reframes capital raising from taboo money-asking to 'providing an opportunity'—pitch deck answers 95% of investor questions; his first deal raised $898K in 3 weeks with no prior fundraising experience

title: How To Start Multi-Family Investing: A Step-By-Step Blueprint For Beginners
